class_schedule.topic: Latin America: Colonial Legacies and Political Trajectories
learning_objectives: Trace colonial legacies and their enduring impact on Latin American politics
Identify the region's key political patterns: populism, military rule, democratization
Analyze the concept of dependency in the Latin American context
readings: Skidmore & Smith, Modern Latin America Ch. 1-2
Galeano, Open Veins of Latin America Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Latin America's political trajectory is shaped by colonial extraction, struggles for independence, and a persistent tension between democracy and authoritarianism.
key_concepts: Colonial legacies: extraction, racial hierarchy, institutional weakness
Independence movements: Bolivar, San Martin, and the creole elite
Caudillismo and the persistence of personalist rule
Import substitution industrialization and its limits
Dependency theory: born in Latin America
lecture_notes.demonstration: Mapping exercise: colonial economic structures and their modern echoes

review.value: The Latin American tradition of charismatic, personalist political leadership combining military authority with populist appeal, from 19th-century strongmen to 21st-century figures
The mid-20th century development strategy of replacing imported manufactured goods with domestic production behind tariff walls, pursued across Latin America with mixed results

learning_objectives: Analyze the third wave of democratization in Latin America
Evaluate current democratic backsliding: Venezuela, Nicaragua, El Salvador
Assess the quality of democracy across the region
readings: Levitsky & Ziblatt, How Democracies Die Ch. 1-2 (excerpt)
Freedom House, Freedom in the Americas (latest)

lecture_notes.introduction: Latin America transitioned to democracy in the 1980s-90s, but many democracies remain fragile and some are eroding from within.
key_concepts: Third wave democratization: military withdrawals, pacted transitions
Delegative democracy: strong presidents, weak institutions (O'Donnell)
Democratic erosion: Venezuela (Chavez/Maduro), Nicaragua (Ortega), El Salvador (Bukele)
Competitive authoritarianism in the hemisphere
Citizen dissatisfaction: democracy's legitimacy crisis in the region
lecture_notes.demonstration: Democratic quality assessment: rating five Latin American countries using V-Dem indicators

review.value: O'Donnell's concept of democracies where elected presidents govern as they see fit, claiming to embody the will of the nation, with weak horizontal accountability
The gradual dismantling of democratic checks and balances from within by elected leaders, rather than through military coups, as seen in Venezuela, Nicaragua, and increasingly El Salvador

class_schedule.topic: Economic Development: From the Lost Decade to the Pink Tide
learning_objectives: Analyze the debt crisis and structural adjustment in the 1980s
Evaluate the Washington Consensus and its backlash
Assess the Pink Tide: left-wing governments and social policy innovation
readings: Edwards, Left Behind: Latin America and the False Promise of Populism Ch. 1 (excerpt)
Lustig, 'The Decline of Inequality in Latin America'

lecture_notes.introduction: Latin America's economic trajectory from debt crisis to commodity boom to stagnation reveals the fundamental challenges of development in the region.
key_concepts: The Lost Decade: debt crisis, hyperinflation, structural adjustment
Washington Consensus in Latin America: liberalization and its consequences
The Pink Tide: left-wing governments from Lula to Morales to Kirchner
Social policy innovations: Bolsa Familia, PROGRESA conditional cash transfers
Commodity dependence and the middle-income trap
lecture_notes.demonstration: Economic data analysis: comparing development indicators before and after the Pink Tide
setup_requirements: Economic time series data for selected countries

review.value: The 1980s debt crisis that devastated Latin American economies, with GDP per capita declining, poverty soaring, and structural adjustment programs imposing austerity and liberalization

The wave of left-leaning governments elected across Latin America (2000s-2010s), pursuing social redistribution, poverty reduction, and greater state role in the economy, funded largely by commodity revenues

class_schedule.topic: Drug Trafficking, Violence, and Citizen Security
learning_objectives: Analyze the political economy of drug trafficking in the Americas
Evaluate different approaches to drug policy: prohibition, decriminalization, legalization
Assess the relationship between organized crime and state weakness
readings: Insight Crime, Organized Crime in Latin America (overview)

lecture_notes.introduction: Drug trafficking and organized crime are among the most devastating challenges facing Latin America, with profound implications for governance, security, and human rights.
key_concepts: Drug trafficking routes and organizations: cartels, networks, territorial control
The balloon effect: why supply-side enforcement displaces but does not eliminate trafficking
State capture and corruption: when criminals infiltrate governments
Citizen security crisis: homicide rates, gang violence, displacement
Policy alternatives: Uruguay's cannabis legalization, Portugal's decriminalization model
lecture_notes.demonstration: Policy debate: evaluating drug legalization versus militarized interdiction

review.value: When drug enforcement pressure in one area causes production or trafficking to shift to another area, like squeezing a balloon -- a fundamental limitation of supply-side drug policy
The process by which criminal organizations infiltrate and corrupt state institutions (police, judiciary, politicians), using bribery, intimidation, and violence to ensure impunity

class_schedule.topic: US-Latin American Relations and Regional Integration
learning_objectives: Analyze the history of US hegemony in the Western Hemisphere
Evaluate regional integration efforts: Mercosur, Pacific Alliance, CELAC
Assess the impact of China's growing presence in Latin America
readings: Smith, Talons of the Eagle Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: The United States has been the dominant external power in Latin America for two centuries. That dominance is now being challenged by China and by Latin American agency.
key_concepts: Monroe Doctrine and its evolution: from anti-colonialism to intervention
Cold War interventions: Guatemala, Chile, Nicaragua, El Salvador
Post-Cold War: democracy promotion, trade agreements, drug war
Regional integration: Mercosur, Pacific Alliance, ALBA, CELAC
China in Latin America: trade, investment, and strategic implications
lecture_notes.demonstration: Historical pattern analysis: US interventions in Latin America and their long-term consequences
setup_requirements: Trade and investment data showing China's rise

review.value: The 1823 US policy declaring the Western Hemisphere off-limits to European colonization, later used to justify US interventions throughout Latin America
China has become Latin America's second-largest trade partner and a major source of loans and investment, offering an alternative to US-dominated institutions and creating a strategic triangle

class_schedule.topic: Contemporary Challenges: Migration, Climate, and Indigenous Rights
learning_objectives: Analyze Central American and Venezuelan migration crises
Evaluate climate vulnerability and environmental governance in the region
Assess indigenous rights movements and their political impact
readings: Yashar, Contesting Citizenship in Latin America Ch. 1 (excerpt)

lecture_notes.introduction: Contemporary Latin America faces interconnected challenges of migration, climate vulnerability, and demands for recognition from historically marginalized indigenous populations.
key_concepts: Northern Triangle migration: push factors, US policy, regional solutions
Venezuelan exodus: the largest displacement crisis in Latin American history
Amazon deforestation: global implications of regional choices
Climate vulnerability: Caribbean islands, Central American dry corridor
Indigenous rights movements: from Bolivia's MAS to Ecuador's CONAIE
lecture_notes.demonstration: Migration policy exercise: designing a comprehensive regional response to the Venezuelan crisis

review.value: Over 7 million Venezuelans have fled economic collapse and political repression since 2015, creating the largest displacement crisis in Latin American history and straining host country resources

Organized political mobilization by indigenous peoples demanding land rights, cultural recognition, and political representation, achieving constitutional recognition in Bolivia, Ecuador, and other countries

questions.question: What is 'delegative democracy' (O'Donnell) and how does it manifest in Latin America? Provide one specific country example.
questions.answer: Delegative democracy is O'Donnell's concept describing a political system where elections are free and fair but elected presidents govern as if they have a personal mandate to rule as they see fit, unconstrained by legislatures, courts, or other horizontal accountability institutions. The president claims to embody the will of the nation and treats institutional checks as obstacles. This manifests in Latin America through presidents governing by decree, packing courts, weakening legislatures, and claiming populist mandates. Example: Nayib Bukele in El Salvador has used his massive popularity to override judicial independence, pack the Supreme Court, extend his term, and deploy the military for domestic law enforcement, while maintaining strong public support by reducing gang violence -- a textbook case of delegative democracy sliding toward authoritarianism.

rubric.key: Explains how it manifests in the region

questions.question: Explain the Washington Consensus and why it generated a political backlash in Latin America that contributed to the Pink Tide.
questions.answer: The Washington Consensus was a set of economic policy prescriptions promoted by the IMF, World Bank, and US Treasury for Latin American countries in the 1990s: fiscal discipline, trade liberalization, privatization of state enterprises, deregulation, and openness to foreign investment. It generated backlash because: liberalization increased inequality even when it produced growth; privatization of utilities and natural resources was perceived as selling national assets to foreign corporations; austerity cut social spending during economic crises, deepening poverty; and structural adjustment was seen as externally imposed by the US and international institutions. This discontent fueled the election of left-wing leaders (Lula in Brazil, Chavez in Venezuela, Morales in Bolivia, Kirchner in Argentina) who promised to reverse neoliberal policies and redistribute wealth -- the Pink Tide.

quizzes.topic: Drug Trafficking, US Relations, and Migration

questions.question: What is the 'balloon effect' in drug policy and what does it imply about the effectiveness of supply-side drug enforcement?
questions.answer: The balloon effect describes how drug enforcement pressure in one area causes production or trafficking to shift to another area, like squeezing a balloon -- the substance moves but doesn't disappear. When the US helped Colombia dismantle the Medellin and Cali cartels, Mexican cartels filled the vacuum. When Mexico cracked down, Central American transit routes expanded. When coca eradication succeeded in one Andean region, cultivation moved elsewhere. This implies that supply-side enforcement alone cannot solve the drug problem because demand remains constant: as long as consumers in the US and Europe want drugs, suppliers will find new routes and production sites. The implication is that demand reduction, treatment, harm reduction, and potentially regulated legalization may be more effective than purely supply-side approaches.

questions.question: How has China's growing presence in Latin America changed the region's relationship with the United States?
questions.answer: China has become Latin America's second-largest trade partner and a major lender, offering an alternative to US-dominated economic institutions. This has changed the US-Latin America dynamic by: (1) reducing US economic leverage -- Latin American countries can turn to China for trade, investment, and loans without political conditionality; (2) creating strategic competition -- the US is concerned about Chinese military-use dual infrastructure (ports, 5G networks) and diplomatic influence (countries switching recognition from Taiwan to Beijing); (3) giving Latin American states more bargaining power -- they can play China and the US against each other to extract better terms; (4) complicating US democracy promotion -- China's engagement model doesn't require governance reforms. However, the US retains advantages in security cooperation, cultural ties, and the presence of large Latin American diaspora communities.

rubric.key: Identifies specific ways China's presence changed dynamics
Shows understanding of both opportunities and concerns

major_assignments.title: Latin American Country Deep Dive
major_assignments.description: Select a Latin American country and produce a comprehensive analysis covering political system, economic development, social dynamics, and international relations. Identify the country's most critical challenge and develop policy recommendations.
objectives: Integrate political, economic, and social analysis
Apply regional knowledge to policy analysis
deliverables: Policy brief on the country's critical challenge

criteria: Comprehensive political, economic, and social analysis
Deep country expertise across multiple dimensions

weekly_quizzes.description: Quizzes on Latin American politics, economics, and current events

participation.description: Engagement in debates, simulations, and policy exercises

major_assignments.description: Country deep dive analysis and presentation

midterm_examination.description: Exam on Latin American history, politics, and development

course_policies.attendance: Regular attendance required. Debate and simulation absences cannot be made up.
course_policies.collaboration: Debate preparation collaborative. Written work individually authored.
course_policies.academic_integrity: All work original with proper citation.
course_policies.technology_requirements: Access to Latin American databases, CEPAL/ECLAC data, and regional media in translation.
